boundedContextsIdentified: crm-core (lead, contact, account, opportunity, task, ticket, billing, feedback)
aggregatesIdentified: CaseDocument (packages/domain/src/legal/cases/case-document.ts) — persisted independently but NOT a formal AggregateRoot (see DDD-007)
NotificationPreference (packages/domain/src/notifications/NotificationPreference.ts) — persisted independently but NOT a formal AggregateRoot (see DDD-008)
meta.summary: Domain layer is genuinely rich for the core CRM aggregates (Lead, Contact, Account, Opportunity, Task): factory methods, Result<T,E> error handling, real Value Objects (Email, PhoneNumber, Money, Percentage, WebsiteUrl), and DRY canonical enums that validators/API derive from. No packages/domain source file imports Prisma, adapters, or apps/* (grep across packages/domain/src for '@prisma/client' / PrismaClient returned zero hits) — the ADR-002/CLAUDE.md 'domain never depends on infrastructure' rule is genuinely honored for the main aggregates. Aggregate-to-aggregate references consistently use raw ID strings (accountId, contactId, sourceLeadId) rather than object references, which is correct DDD practice. The gaps found cluster around three themes: (1) transactional integrity of domain-event publishing and multi-aggregate use cases does not match what ADR-011 and the code's own comments claim (events published in a separate, non-atomic call after aggregate persistence, with failures silently swallowed via console.error; a 4-aggregate lead-conversion flow is documented as atomic but has zero transaction wrapping); (2) a handful of business invariants/policies (auto-qualify/auto-disqualify thresholds) live in the Application layer instead of the aggregate, and a handful of domain-shaped concepts (CaseDocument, NotificationPreference) are persisted independently via their own repositories but are not implemented as formal AggregateRoot/Entity types and never emit real domain events despite having event-shaped interfaces defined; (3) documentation drift — docs/planning/DDD-context-map.puml and ADR-002's package-structure section do not reflect roughly half of the bounded contexts that actually exist in packages/domain/src today (most notably the entire Legal/Case-Management context, which ADR-002's own problem statement names as one of four core contexts but which is absent from both documents). A missing TenantId value object is also notable given this project's documented history of tenant-isolation incidents (see MEMORY.md: 'Tenant-leak provisioning incident', 'Prod-DB guard incident').

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: File docstring at line 97 states '8. Persists all changes atomically', but the method mutates and saves FOUR separate aggregates via four independent, non-transactional repository calls with no shared transaction: Account.save at line 252 (inside handleAccount, with its own try/catch at 251-257 and a separate best-effort event publish at 262-268), Contact.save at line 299 (inside createContactFromLead, own try/catch 298-304, own best-effort publish 309-315), and finally Opportunity.save + Lead.save together at lines 176-177 inside a third, later try/catch (175-182). `grep -n "transaction\|\$transaction\|withTransaction" packages/application/src/usecases/leads/ConvertLeadToDealUseCase.ts` returns zero matches — there is no transaction boundary anywhere in the file. The same non-atomic pattern (Account.save, then later Contact.save + Lead.save, as two separate try/catch blocks) is independently duplicated in packages/application/src/services/LeadService.ts convertLead() at lines 344-358 (account) and 386-392 (contact+lead).
findings.impact: If the process crashes or a later save throws after an earlier one has committed (e.g. Opportunity.save at line 176 succeeds but Lead.save at line 177 throws), the result is an orphaned Account/Contact/Opportunity in the database while the source Lead remains QUALIFIED (not CONVERTED) — allowing duplicate conversion attempts, orphaned pipeline data, and a broken audit trail. This directly contradicts the method's own documented atomicity guarantee and the aggregate-boundary premise of DDD (each aggregate is its own consistency boundary; cross-aggregate consistency should be handled via compensating actions or eventual consistency via events, not assumed multi-repository atomicity that doesn't exist).
findings.rootCause: The use case was written assuming repository.save() calls inside sequential try/catch blocks provide atomicity; no packages/db withTransaction/$transaction wrapper was used to span the Account+Contact+Opportunity+Lead writes.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-002 (aggregates as consistency boundaries) / DDD aggregate transactional-integrity convention
findings.remediation: Either (a) wrap the whole conversion in a single packages/db withTransaction call so all four aggregate writes commit or roll back together (requires each Prisma*Repository to accept an optional TransactionClient), or (b) redesign as a saga: persist Lead-conversion-intent first, then create Account/Contact/Opportunity idempotently with compensating deletes on failure, and only flip Lead to CONVERTED after all downstream aggregates exist. Do not claim atomicity in the docstring until one of these is implemented.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: lines 69-84 — `await this.leadRepository.save(lead)` (a full, separately-committed Prisma write) completes and returns BEFORE `await this.eventBus.publishAll(events)` is invoked (lines 78-83), and any publish failure is caught and only `console.error`'d, never surfaced or retried: `catch (error) { console.error('Failed to publish domain events:', error); }`. The identical pattern (save, then separate best-effort publishAll wrapped in try/catch that only logs) repeats in packages/application/src/services/LeadService.ts publishEvents() at lines 698-708, and in ConvertLeadToDealUseCase.ts publishEvents() at lines 370-387. Even though packages/adapters/src/events/OutboxEventBusAdapter.ts publishAll() (lines 110-186) itself uses `withTransaction` for the outbox-table INSERTs, that transaction is a separate DB transaction from the one (if any) used by PrismaLeadRepository.save().
findings.impact: Violates ADR-011's core promise ('Zero Lost Events... Events stored atomically with domain state changes... No dual-write problem'). If the process crashes, times out, or the outbox write fails between the aggregate save and the event publish, the state change is persisted but no LeadCreated/LeadQualified/LeadConverted event ever reaches the outbox — silently breaking any downstream consumer (audit log per ADR-008, notifications, AI scoring triggers, analytics) with no retry and no alert (just a console.error that is easily lost in production logs).
findings.rootCause: The outbox pattern (ADR-011) was implemented as an adapter capable of atomic multi-event writes, but the call sites in the application layer never invoke it inside the same transaction as the aggregate's own repository.save(), and error handling treats event publishing as 'best-effort' rather than required.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-011 Domain Events Infrastructure — 'Zero Lost Events' KPI and transactional outbox guarantee
findings.remediation: Extend LeadRepository.save (and sibling repositories) to accept an optional TransactionClient, and have each use case open one transaction that both persists the aggregate and writes the outbox rows, e.g. `withTransaction(tx => { leadRepo.save(lead, tx); outbox.publishAll(events, tx); })`. Remove the swallow-and-log catch blocks once this is atomic — a failure should fail the whole use case.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `tenantId: string;` appears as a raw, unvalidated primitive in every aggregate's props interface — Lead.ts:65, packages/domain/src/crm/contact/Contact.ts:81, packages/domain/src/crm/account/Account.ts:71, packages/domain/src/crm/opportunity/Opportunity.ts:74 (163 occurrences of the literal `tenantId: string` across 49 files per `grep -rn "tenantId: string" packages/domain/src | wc -l`). `find packages/domain/src -iname "*tenant*"` returns only CrossTenantOrNotFoundError.ts (an error type, not a value object) — no TenantId class exists anywhere in packages/domain/src/shared or elsewhere, unlike Email, PhoneNumber, Money, Percentage, WebsiteUrl which all have dedicated Value Objects with Result-returning factories.
findings.impact: TenantId is the single most safety-critical field in a multi-tenant SaaS domain (the isolation boundary between customers), yet it has zero domain-level validation (format, non-empty, non-placeholder) and can be silently swapped for an empty string, another tenant's id, or a literal default value anywhere a plain string is expected. This project has direct incident history in this exact area (see docs/operations and MEMORY.md entries 'Tenant-leak provisioning incident' and 'Prod-DB guard incident') that a TenantId VO with compile-time type distinction (vs. plain string) and a validating factory would materially reduce the risk surface of.
findings.rootCause: tenantId was modeled as a primitive from the start of the domain layer and every subsequent aggregate followed the same (unvalidated) pattern; packages/domain/CLAUDE.md even documents 'Multi-tenancy: Almost all models need tenantId' without ever proposing a VO for it.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-002 tactical DDD patterns (Value Objects ensure invariants at compile-time) / packages/domain/CLAUDE.md multi-tenancy note
findings.remediation: Introduce a TenantId value object in packages/domain/src/shared/TenantId.ts (mirroring Email/PhoneNumber's Result-returning static create()), and incrementally migrate aggregate props from `tenantId: string` to `tenantId: TenantId`, starting with the highest-risk aggregates (Lead, Contact, Account, Opportunity, Invoice). This is a larger multi-sprint migration; recommend landing the VO + Lead first as a template.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: LEAD_SCORE_THRESHOLDS (HOT: 80, WARM: 50, AUTO_QUALIFY: 75, AUTO_DISQUALIFY: 20) is defined as an application-layer constant at lines 22-28. The auto-qualification POLICY itself — 'if a fresh AI score crosses a threshold, automatically call lead.qualify() or lead.changeStatus()' — is implemented as raw if/else in updateLeadScore() at lines 227-244: `if (score >= LEAD_SCORE_THRESHOLDS.AUTO_QUALIFY && lead.status === 'NEW') { lead.qualify(...) } else if (score <= LEAD_SCORE_THRESHOLDS.AUTO_DISQUALIFY && lead.status === 'NEW') { lead.changeStatus('UNQUALIFIED', ...) }`. The Lead aggregate's own qualify()/changeStatus() methods (Lead.ts:334-349, 320-332) only validate state-transition legality (not-already-converted, correct source status) — they have no concept of 'should I auto-qualify based on score', so this policy only fires for callers that happen to go through LeadService.updateLeadScore().
findings.impact: The 'automatically (dis)qualify based on AI score' business rule is a domain policy but is not reachable from the Lead aggregate itself — any other caller that legitimately updates a lead's score (a different use case, a batch job, a future API path) will silently skip auto-(dis)qualification unless it independently re-implements the same threshold check. The thresholds (75/20) are also close-but-different from the tier boundaries already encoded in LeadScore.tier (80/50, packages/domain/src/crm/lead/LeadScore.ts:47-51), so the domain and application layers each hard-code their own version of 'what counts as a good/bad score' with no single source of truth.
findings.rootCause: The policy was implemented directly in the service method that happens to also call the AI scoring port, rather than being expressed as an aggregate method (e.g. `lead.applyScoreAndAutoQualify(score, confidence, thresholds)`) or a standalone domain policy object that any use case can invoke consistently.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-002 'Rich Domain Model: Business logic lives in domain entities, not services'
findings.remediation: Move the auto-qualify/auto-disqualify decision into the Lead aggregate (e.g. a `Lead.applyAiScore(score, confidence, modelVersion, thresholds)` method that internally calls updateScore + conditionally qualify/changeStatus and emits a single coherent event), or at minimum extract a pure domain policy function co-located with LeadScore so every caller shares the same thresholds. Reconcile the 75/20 auto-qualify bounds with LeadScore.tier's 80/50 tier bounds or document why they intentionally differ.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `private static readonly SUPPORTED_CURRENCIES = ['GBP', 'EUR', 'GBP', 'CAD', 'AUD', 'JPY'];` — 'GBP' is listed twice and 'USD' is absent entirely. `Money.create(100, 'USD')` therefore fails with InvalidMoneyError('Unsupported currency: USD...') via the check at line 74. Meanwhile packages/validators/src/report-settings.ts line 60 sets `DEFAULT_REPORT_SETTINGS.currency = 'USD'` as the system default, and its currencyCodeSchema (lines 9-12) is a generic 3-letter-regex validator that accepts ANY ISO-shaped code, not just Money's supported list — so the validators layer and the domain Money VO disagree about which currencies are valid.
findings.impact: Any code path that constructs Money for a USD amount (e.g. a US-based tenant's Opportunity, Invoice, or the report-settings default flowing into a monetary calculation) will hit a domain validation failure that reads as 'unsupported currency' even though USD is a completely standard currency and is literally the documented default elsewhere in the same codebase.
findings.rootCause: Typo/copy-paste error when the SUPPORTED_CURRENCIES array was authored — 'USD' was almost certainly intended and 'GBP' was accidentally duplicated instead.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-002 Value Objects ensure invariants — invariant itself is defective
findings.remediation: Fix the array to `['GBP', 'USD', 'EUR', 'CAD', 'AUD', 'JPY']`, add a unit test asserting each currency in packages/validators' allowed set round-trips through Money.create, and add a DRY-enum-style single-source-of-truth check (similar to enum-consistency.spec.ts) so Money.SUPPORTED_CURRENCIES and any validator-side currency list can't silently diverge again.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `function computeWeightedValue(opps) { ... return sum + value * probability; }` (lines 502-508) and `function buildStageBreakdown(...)` (lines 464-478, `breakdown[opp.stage].weightedValue += Number(opp.value) * (opp.probability / 100)`) reimplement, on raw Prisma query rows, exactly the calculation already encapsulated in the Opportunity aggregate's own `get weightedValue(): Money` getter (packages/domain/src/crm/opportunity/Opportunity.ts:185-190, `this.props.value.amount * this.props.probability.asDecimal`). The router additionally hardcodes currency at line 657 (`weightedValue: { amount: numericValue * (existing.probability / 100), currency: 'GBP' }`) instead of reading it from the persisted Money/currency, and repeats the same raw arithmetic again at lines 780-786 and 1277-1283.
findings.impact: Pipeline/forecast numbers (weightedValue, totalPipelineValue, forecast accuracy) are computed by a hand-rolled formula operating on raw DB rows rather than by reconstituting Opportunity aggregates and calling the canonical `.weightedValue` getter, so any future change to the domain's weighting/rounding logic (e.g. Money's Math.round-to-cents behavior) will silently NOT apply to these router-computed figures, and any tenant using a non-GBP currency gets a mislabeled 'GBP' amount in the forecast/stage-breakdown responses regardless of their Opportunity's actual currency.
findings.rootCause: The analytics/forecast endpoints were built for performance against raw Prisma aggregate queries (`_sum`, `_avg`) rather than loading full Opportunity domain entities, and the weighting formula was re-derived inline instead of being reused from the domain.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-002 hexagonal boundary — domain calculations should not be re-implemented in the API layer
findings.remediation: Either expose a lightweight pure function in the domain (e.g. `Opportunity.calculateWeightedValue(amount, currency, probability)`) that both the aggregate getter and these router aggregate-query helpers call, or restrict router-level analytics to genuinely bulk-only Prisma aggregation and read currency per-row instead of hardcoding 'GBP'.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `export class CaseDocument { private constructor(private readonly data: CaseDocumentData) {} ... }` at line 201 does not extend Entity or AggregateRoot (`grep -n "extends Entity|extends AggregateRoot" packages/domain/src/legal/cases/case-document.ts` returns no matches), so it has no `addDomainEvent`/`getDomainEvents` capability. Yet it is persisted through its own independent repository contract (`export interface CaseDocumentRepository` at line 560, backed by packages/application/src/ports/repositories/CaseDocumentRepositoryPort.ts and packages/adapters/src/repositories/PrismaCaseDocumentRepository.ts) rather than being loaded/saved only as part of the Case aggregate. The file defines event-shaped interfaces — DocumentCreatedEvent, DocumentVersionedEvent, DocumentAccessGrantedEvent, DocumentSignedEvent, DocumentArchivedEvent, DocumentDeletedEvent (lines 609-644) — but `grep -rn "DocumentSignedEvent|DocumentCreatedEvent|DocumentArchivedEvent|DocumentVersionedEvent" packages/` shows these interfaces are referenced ONLY inside case-document.ts and DocumentIngestionEvents.ts, never instantiated or passed to any EventBusPort/outbox — they are dead contracts.
findings.impact: Case documents are a GDPR- and e-signature-relevant surface (the file's own header says 'access control, and audit trails... e-signature workflows, retention policies, and GDPR compliance'), yet signing, archiving, versioning, and deleting a case document never emits a real domain event through the outbox — meaning ADR-008 audit logging and any cross-context consumer (notifications, compliance) cannot react to these actions via the documented event-driven mechanism the rest of the codebase relies on.
findings.rootCause: CaseDocument was modeled with its own Zod-validated data class and a self-contained repository before/independently of the AggregateRoot pattern being applied here, and the event interfaces were sketched as documentation of intended events without being wired into the real DomainEvent base class or outbox adapter.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-011 Domain Events Infrastructure / ADR-008 Audit Logging (uses domain events for audit trail)
findings.remediation: Either fold CaseDocument lifecycle operations into the Case aggregate (Case as the sole aggregate root, CaseDocument as an internal entity only mutated through Case) or, if CaseDocument must remain independently persisted for practical reasons (large binary metadata, high write volume), promote it to a proper AggregateRoot<CaseDocumentId> and rewire its DocumentSignedEvent/DocumentArchivedEvent/etc. to extend the real DomainEvent base class and flow through OutboxEventBusAdapter like every other aggregate's events.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `export class NotificationPreference extends Entity<string> {` at line 74 (not AggregateRoot). It has a dedicated NotificationPreferenceRepository (packages/domain/src/notifications/NotificationPreferenceRepository.ts) implying it is loaded/saved as its own root, and it exposes a classic bag-of-setters mutation surface: setChannelEnabled (170), setChannelFrequency (181), setChannelVerified (192), setCategoryEnabled (210), setQuietHours (223), setQuietHoursEnabled (236), setQuietHoursDays (244), setTimezone (256), setDoNotDisturb (267) — `grep -c "addDomainEvent" packages/domain/src/notifications/NotificationPreference.ts` returns 0.
findings.impact: Because NotificationPreference extends the bare Entity class, it structurally cannot call addDomainEvent even if someone wanted it to — every preference mutation (opting out of a channel, enabling Do-Not-Disturb, changing quiet hours) is invisible to the rest of the system via the domain-event mechanism. Any consumer that needs to react to preference changes (e.g. a digest/reminder scheduler that should stop sending once DND is enabled) must poll or re-query rather than subscribe to an event.
findings.rootCause: NotificationPreference was modeled as a plain Entity (has identity, no events) even though it is persisted and mutated independently like an aggregate root.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-002 tactical pattern (repository-backed roots should be AggregateRoot with domain events)
findings.remediation: Change `extends Entity<string>` to `extends AggregateRoot<string>` and add addDomainEvent calls to the setter methods that represent meaningful business changes (at minimum setDoNotDisturb and setChannelEnabled, since those directly affect whether other contexts should send notifications).
findings.falsePositiveJustification: It is plausible this was a deliberate, low-priority simplification (preferences are typically read synchronously by the sender at send-time rather than reacted to asynchronously), so the 'missing event' impact may be smaller than for a transactional aggregate. Still worth confirming with the notifications context owner.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: The entire docs/planning/DDD-context-map.puml (129 lines) documents exactly 4 packages — 'CRM Core Domain' (Lead/Contact/Account/Opportunity/Ticket), 'Intelligence Domain' (Scoring/RAG/Model Governance), 'Platform & Support Domain' (IAM/Documents/Workflow/Audit), and 'Integration Domain' (Communication/ERP) — with no mention of 'Legal', 'Case', 'Appointment', or 'Deadline' anywhere in the file. Yet `packages/domain/src/legal/` contains cases/ (Case.ts, CaseEvents.ts, CaseTask.ts, case-document.ts, DocumentIngestionEvents.ts), appointments/ (Appointment.ts, ConflictDetector.ts, Recurrence.ts, TimeSlot.ts, Buffer.ts), and deadlines/ (Deadline.ts, DeadlineRule.ts, HolidayCalendar.ts, deadline-engine.ts) — a substantial, actively developed bounded context. ADR-002 itself names 'Case Management' as one of exactly four core bounded contexts in its Context and Problem Statement (line 13: 'multiple bounded contexts (CRM, Case Management, Intelligence, Platform)'), but ADR-002's own 'Package Structure' section (lines 526-564) and the context-map .puml agree with each other while both omit Case Management entirely. Also absent from the map: Billing (crm/billing), Notifications, Automation, Autoresponse, Activity-Feed, Support, Timeline, Security, AI-Review — all present as directories under packages/domain/src.
findings.impact: New contributors and AI agents (this repo explicitly optimizes for 'AI-Assisted Development... Clear patterns make AI code generation safer', ADR-002 line 30) using the context map or ADR-002 as their mental model of the system will not know Legal/Case-Management exists as a bounded context, cannot reason about its relationships/coupling to CRM Core (e.g. does a Case reference a Contact/Account? by what pattern?), and may inadvertently introduce improper cross-context coupling because the intended integration pattern (Conformist / Customer-Supplier / ACL, per the map's own legend) was never decided for it.
findings.rootCause: The context map and ADR-002 were authored early (2025-12-21) before the Legal/Case-Management context, Billing, and several other packages/domain/src subdirectories existed, and neither document has been updated since as the domain grew.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-002 'Bounded Contexts' decision driver — context map should reflect actual architecture
findings.remediation: Regenerate docs/planning/DDD-context-map.puml (and its rendered IntelliFlow-CRM-Context-Map.png) to add a 'Legal Case Management' package with Case/CaseDocument/Appointment/Deadline, plus at minimum Billing and Notifications as first-class boxes, and explicitly decide + document each one's relationship arrows to CRM Core per the existing legend (Conformist/Customer-Supplier/ACL/Partnership).

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `recordToLeadProps()` at lines 47-78 sets `score: { value: record.score, confidence: 1, // Prisma stores only the score value; default confidence }` (lines 69-72) on every single reconstitution from persistence, regardless of what confidence the AI scoring actually produced. The file's own header comment at lines 84-85 acknowledges this: 'Prisma schema only has score (Int), not separate value/confidence. We store score value and use default confidence (1.0) when reconstituting.'
findings.impact: LeadScore.confidence (packages/domain/src/crm/lead/LeadScore.ts:43-45, `get isHighConfidence(): boolean { return this.props.confidence >= 0.8; }`) is a real domain concept that any freshly-scored-and-saved-then-reloaded Lead silently loses — every reload reports confidence=1 (isHighConfidence always true) even if the AI model that scored it reported low confidence. Any feature relying on isHighConfidence after a reload (not immediately after scoring, e.g. a later page load, a batch job, an export) will get a wrong answer.
findings.rootCause: The Prisma `Lead.score` column is a plain Int with no companion confidence column, so confidence cannot round-trip through persistence at all.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-002 'Type Safety: Value Objects ensure invariants at compile-time' — the invariant (confidence 0-1) is preserved at compile-time but silently falsified at runtime on every DB round-trip
findings.remediation: Add a `scoreConfidence Float?` column to the Prisma Lead model (migration), persist LeadScore.confidence alongside LeadScore.value in PrismaLeadRepository.save(), and read it back in recordToLeadProps() instead of hardcoding 1.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `updateContactInfo(props)` at lines 368-398 mutates up to 15 fields (firstName, lastName, company, title, phone, location, website, avatarUrl, lastContactedAt, estimatedValue, tags, budget, authority, need, timeline, annualRevenue) via `Object.assign(this.props, ...)` (lines 393-396) and never calls `this.addDomainEvent(...)`. `grep -n "^export class" packages/domain/src/crm/lead/LeadEvents.ts` confirms only LeadCreatedEvent, LeadScoredEvent, LeadStatusChangedEvent, LeadQualifiedEvent, LeadConvertedEvent, and LeadRoutedEvent exist — there is no LeadUpdatedEvent at all. Contrast with the structurally identical Contact.updateContactInfo() (packages/domain/src/crm/contact/Contact.ts:323-358), which DOES track `updatedFields` and DOES call `this.addDomainEvent(new ContactUpdatedEvent(this.id, updatedFields, updatedBy))` at line 354.
findings.impact: Editing a lead's name, company, BANT qualification data (budget/authority/need/timeline), estimated value, or tags — all business-meaningful changes for a sales-pipeline entity — produces zero audit trail via the domain-event mechanism, unlike the equivalent Contact operation. Any consumer expecting to react to 'lead was updated' (audit log per ADR-008, activity-feed, real-time UI sync) cannot, for this class of change, and there is no way to add one without first adding a LeadUpdatedEvent class that doesn't currently exist.
findings.rootCause: Lead.updateContactInfo appears to have been added later (Lead 360 / BANT fields, per the inline comments referencing IFC-242) as a bulk-update convenience method without following the ContactUpdatedEvent precedent already established in the sibling Contact aggregate.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-002 Domain Events pattern; CLAUDE.md 'All cross-context communication MUST go through domain events'
findings.remediation: Add a LeadUpdatedEvent class to LeadEvents.ts (mirroring ContactUpdatedEvent's shape: id, updatedFields, updatedBy) and call `this.addDomainEvent(new LeadUpdatedEvent(this.id, updatedFields, updatedBy))` from Lead.updateContactInfo when at least one field actually changed.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: Two independent code paths implement 'convert a qualified Lead' with materially different behavior: LeadService.convertLead() (LeadService.ts:313-405) creates only an Account + Contact and has NO dealValue validation, while ConvertLeadToDealUseCase.execute() (packages/application/src/usecases/leads/ConvertLeadToDealUseCase.ts:109-199) creates an Account + Contact + Opportunity and requires `input.dealValue > 0` (line 208) plus a `lead.status !== 'QUALIFIED'` gate with the specific comment '// 4.5. QUALIFIED gate — only qualified leads can be converted to deals (AC-008)' (line 136). LeadService.convertLead has the equivalent check at line 329 ('Only qualified leads can be converted') — same rule, independently re-typed in two files.
findings.impact: Two call paths enforce overlapping-but-not-identical versions of the lead-conversion invariant (e.g. only ConvertLeadToDealUseCase validates dealValue; only it creates an Opportunity). If a router or scheduled job is wired to the older LeadService.convertLead path instead of the newer use case (or vice versa), behavior and validation coverage silently differ for what is conceptually the same business operation, and a future rule change (e.g. tightening the QUALIFIED gate) must be remembered and applied in both places.
findings.rootCause: ConvertLeadToDealUseCase appears to be a newer, more complete implementation (FLOW-006 / IFC-062) added alongside the pre-existing LeadService.convertLead rather than as a replacement for it.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-002 'Rich Domain Model' — a single business capability should have one authoritative implementation
findings.remediation: Determine which of the two is actually wired to the lead.router.ts conversion endpoint, deprecate/remove the other (or have LeadService.convertLead delegate to ConvertLeadToDealUseCase internally), and consolidate the QUALIFIED-status gate into a single Lead aggregate method so both call paths (if both must remain) share the same enforcement.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `ownerId: string;` (Lead.ts:64) and the equivalent field in Contact/Account/Opportunity/Task are plain strings with no format validation, same pattern as tenantId (DDD-003) but lower blast radius since ownerId errors manifest as an assignment bug rather than a tenant-isolation breach.
findings.impact: A malformed or empty ownerId can be assigned to any aggregate without a domain-level rejection; incorrect owner assignment would only surface downstream (e.g. a Prisma foreign-key error, or silently as an orphaned-owner record if the FK isn't enforced).
findings.rootCause: Same historical pattern as tenantId — IDs modeled as raw strings from the start.
findings.remediation: Lower priority than DDD-003; consider a shared UserId VO reused for both ownerId and assigneeId once TenantId is introduced, for consistency.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `Object.assign(this.props, Object.fromEntries(Object.entries(props).filter(([, v]) => v !== undefined)))` at lines 393-396 is a single generic 'set whatever fields were passed' operation rather than intention-revealing methods (e.g. updateBantQualification(), updateContactDetails(), addTags()). It bypasses any per-field invariant checks that might otherwise live on individual setters (contrast with Contact's applyScalarUpdates/applyPhoneUpdate helper methods at Contact.ts:360-420, which at least validate phone via PhoneNumber.create before assignment).
findings.impact: Low risk today since inputs are typed (Partial<Pick<LeadProps,...>>), but this pattern makes it easy for future fields to be added to the bulk-update surface without ever having a validation checkpoint, and makes it hard to reason about which specific business operation a given call represents when reading calling code or event logs (compounded by DDD-011's lack of an emitted event).
findings.rootCause: Convenience/velocity trade-off when Lead 360 fields were added in bulk (IFC-242 comment references).
findings.remediation: Not urgent; if/when DDD-011 (LeadUpdatedEvent) is addressed, consider splitting updateContactInfo into a couple of named methods (updateProfile, updateQualificationData) for clarity, though this is a style preference rather than a correctness issue.
findings.falsePositiveJustification: This is a stylistic/maintainability observation more than a DDD-correctness defect; the method IS type-safe and does validate via the LeadProps type system even without per-call invariant checks.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `grep -rln "@prisma/client\|PrismaClient" packages/domain/src` returns zero matches — no file under packages/domain/src imports Prisma or any infrastructure package. Core aggregates (Lead, Contact, Account, Opportunity, Task) consistently reference sibling aggregates only by raw ID string (e.g. Opportunity.accountId: string, Opportunity.ts:70; Contact.accountId: string, Contact.ts:78) rather than by object reference, and all five have Result<T,DomainError>-returning factories plus real Value Objects (Email, PhoneNumber, Money, Percentage, WebsiteUrl) with validating static create() methods.
findings.impact: This is a genuine strength: the 'domain never depends on infrastructure' rule from ADR-002/packages/domain/CLAUDE.md is actually enforced in practice for the core CRM aggregates, and the architecture-tests package (referenced in packages/domain/CLAUDE.md) has evidently kept this boundary clean over time despite the codebase's size (~230+ files touched in this audit's sampling).
findings.remediation: No action needed; worth calling out so remediation of DDD-001 through DDD-014 is understood as targeted fixes to specific gaps rather than a wholesale architecture problem.
